title: Working with Nested Lists and List Functions
description: Handling lists within lists, and useful functions like str(), lapply(), names().

slides.title: Nested Lists 🪆
slides.content:
Lists can contain other lists. This is called nesting.

```r
nested <- list(a = 1:3, b = list(x = "inner", y = c(TRUE, FALSE)))
```

Accessing nested elements requires multiple brackets: `nested$b$x` or `nested[["b"]][["x"]]`.

slides.title: Nested List Example
slides.codeSnippet:
nested <- list(person = list(name = "Alice", age = 30), scores = c(85,92))
print(nested$person$name)
print(nested[["person"]][["age"]])

# Add deeper nesting
nested$person$address <- list(city = "Paris", zip = 75001)
str(nested)

quizData.question: Given `l <- list(a = list(b = 2, c = 3))`, how do you access the value 2?
quizData.explanation: All three are valid: l$a$b, l[["a"]][["b"]], l[['a']]$b work.

slides.title: Useful List Functions
slides.content:
- `names(l)` – get or set component names.
- `length(l)` – number of top-level components.
- `str(l)` – display structure.
- `lapply(l, FUN)` – apply a function to each component, returns a list.
- `sapply(l, FUN)` – simplify result to vector or matrix if possible.

slides.title: lapply Example
slides.codeSnippet:
l <- list(a = 1:5, b = 6:10, c = 11:15)
means <- lapply(l, mean)
print(means)

# sapply simplifies to a vector
print(sapply(l, mean))

quizData.question: What does `lapply(list(1:3, 4:6), sum)` return?
quizData.explanation: lapply always returns a list, so list(6,15). sapply would return a vector.
